raw_case:
Leukemic occult infiltrative optic neuropathy presenting as optic neuritis: a case report
Isolated ocular relapses of leukemia are rare and typically present as infiltrative lesions of the optic nerve. This report presents a case of an isolated ocular relapse of leukemia in a female patient, characterized by occult infiltrative optic neuropathy. Despite the absence of leukemic cell infiltration on MRI, a 33-year-old female patient presented with optic neuritis, which resolved after intrathecal administration of chemotherapeutic agents. However, due to her non-compliance with the treatment regimen, leukemia progressed to infiltrative optic neuropathy. She refused radiation therapy, and while intrathecal injections provided temporary stabilization, they were insufficient for sustained disease control. This case uniquely documents the extended progression of an isolated ocular relapse of leukemia, evolving from an occult infiltrative lesion initially presenting as optic neuritis to a more pronounced infiltrative lesion. It suggests that leukemic optic nerve infiltration can occur in patients with leukemia even in the absence of MRI-detectable abnormalities. Radiation therapy emerges as a critical modality in the management of infiltrative lesions of the optic nerve.

Introduction

The optic nerve is a known site for potential central nervous system (CNS) relapse in leukemia. Approximately 17–34% of leukemias will present with optic nerve involvement as infiltrative lesions [1]. Leukemic infiltrative optic neuropathy is defined by subacute or acute vision loss due to leukemic cell infiltration in the optic nerve and its sheath. Although leukemia relapse often affects multiple organs, isolated ocular relapse accounts for only 2.2% of cases [2]. To date, there is no literature reporting occult infiltrative optic neuropathy presenting as optic neuritis and manifesting as isolated ocular relapse in leukemia. We report a case of optic neuropathy during the remission phase of acute B-lymphoblastic leukemia, progressing from occult infiltrative optic neuropathy to infiltrative optic neuropathy.

Case presentation

A 33-year-old woman was diagnosed with acute B-lymphoblastic leukemia (BCR/ABL-P190+) in 2019 and underwent allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) following three cycles of chemotherapy. Post-transplant, she received immunosuppressive therapy to prevent graft-versus-host disease, and the leukemia entered complete remission. In 2021, she presented with rapid loss of visual acuity and eye pain in both eyes for 10 days. Her visual acuity was 20/200 in the right eye (RE) and light perception in the left eye (LE). The pupillary light reflex was weaker than normal bilaterally. Ophthalmoscopy revealed bilateral optic disc edema and tortuous retinal vessels. Automated visual field testing identified blind spot enlargement with a ring scotoma in RE, and the LE could not be examined because of low vision. MRI showed demyelination of the cerebral white matter and thickening of the bilateral optic nerves without enhancement (Fig. 1). Hematologic and cerebrospinal fluid tests for infectious and inflammatory etiologies were negative results (including AQP4 antibodies, MOG antibodies, CRMP5 antibodies, etc.). There were no signs of CNS involvement other than the optic nerve. She declined an optic nerve biopsy. Suspecting isolated ocular relapse of leukemia causing bilateral optic neuritis, she received an intrathecal injection of dexamethasone (5 mg), which alleviated ocular pain but did not improve vision. She refused further treatment and was subsequently treated at another hospital for optic neuritis with high-dose methylprednisolone pulse therapy, which restored vision to 20/20 in both eyes. Subsequently, she had an intermittent visual loss, which was restored each time after hormone therapy at other hospitals. Her leukemia remained in remission during the period.
By January 2023, she noticed vision loss and hormone therapy was ineffective. The corrected visual acuity was 20/400 in RE and 20/25 in LE, with a relative afferent papillary defect in RE. Ophthalmoscopy revealed a pale optic disc and thin arteries. The visual evoked potential (VEP) showed prolonged P100 latency bilaterally, a normal P100 amplitude, and reduced amplitude on LE compared with RE. CSF examination remained unremarkable. However, she found hormone therapy was ineffective. Six months later, she presented to our hospital with visual acuity of light perception in RE and 20/40 in LE. MRI showed bilateral thickening and enhancement of the optic nerves and cerebral white matter, with abnormal signals and soft meninges. CSF analysis remained normal. Her right optic nerve appeared atrophied. VEP revealed no waveform in RE, while LE showed near-normal P-VEP latency and reduced amplitude at high spatial frequencies. She was diagnosed with isolated ocular relapse and leukemic infiltrative optic neuropathy. Orbital radiotherapy was recommended, but she did not perform it. She received intrathecal injections of chemotherapeutic agents (cytarabine 30 mg, methotrexate 10 mg and dexamethasone 5 mg) and her vision stabilized. She then underwent regular intrathecal injections with no improvement in vision in both eyes.In early 2024, the patient returned for right eye proptosis with pain for 10 days. The visual acuity was no light perception in RE and 20/40 in LE. On examination, RE protruded markedly with RAPD+. The proptosis was 22 mm in RE and 19 mm in LE. MRI showed bilateral optic nerve infiltration, prominent on the right side, and multiple abnormal signals with nodular enhancement in both cerebral hemispheres (Fig. 2). The CSF contained 41.3% abnormal primitive cells, some suspicious tumor cells on pathology. She was diagnosed with the progression of leukemic infiltrative optic neuropathy. Three weeks later, her vision in both eyes decreased to no light perception. On fundus examination, the optic discs in both eyes were elevated, hemorrhages were visible in the right retina and the optic nerve was atrophied in LE (Fig. 2). Several months later, she died of leukemic multisystem involvement.


Discussion/conclusion

The optic nerve represents a potential site for leukemia recurrence, with the primary mechanisms of involvement including direct invasion and damage by leukemia cells, secondary inflammatory injury, nutrient deprivation, and compressive effects [3]. Clinically, optic nerve relapse in leukemia is predominantly characterized by infiltrative lesions, frequently detected via MRI scans [4–6]. To date, there has been no report detailing the progression from occult infiltrative optic neuropathy with initial optic neuritis symptoms to an isolated ocular relapse of leukemia, manifested as infiltrative optic neuropathy.In this case, the initial ocular abnormality during the remission phase of the patient’s leukemia presented as optic neuritis. MRI scans showed no leukemic cell infiltration, and all diagnostic tests returned negative results. The symptoms subsided following intrathecal injection therapy. The insidious infiltration of leukemic cells into the optic nerve ultimately led to the development of optic neuritis. Hormonal shock therapy administered at an external facility was only effective against inflammation and did not reduce the number of leukemic cells infiltrating the optic nerves. As the leukemic cell invasion of the optic nerve intensified, MRI findings revealed bilateral thickening and enhancement of the optic nerve, indicative of leukemic optic nerve infiltration. The patient did not comply with the recommended treatment, leading to the eventual detection of leukemic cells in her cerebrospinal fluid. She had already missed the optimal therapeutic window.Chemotherapeutic agents have been implicated in causing nerve demyelination and infectious optic neuropathy [7]. However, chemotherapy had been discontinued for over two years when the patient’s initial symptoms appeared, and tests for infectious etiology consistently returned negative. Optic neuritis can also result from chronic graft-versus-host disease (cGVHD) following HSCT. Typically, cGVHD less frequently affects the central nervous system and manifests as inflammatory demyelinating lesions [8]. In this patient, the MRI two years post-transplantation displayed bilateral cerebral white matter demyelination and bilateral optic nerve thickening without enhancement. There were no inflammatory cerebrospinal fluid manifestations or other central nervous system symptoms, thereby ruling out CNS lesions due to cGVHD.This case is the first to describe the prolonged development of an isolated ocular relapse of leukemia from an occult infiltrating lesion, initially presenting as optic neuritis, to a full-fledged infiltrative lesion. This suggests that leukemic optic nerve infiltration may occur in leukemia patients even in the absence of MRI-detectable abnormalities, underscoring its significant implications for clinical management. The combined use of intrathecal injection and radiation therapy is vital in treating infiltrative optic nerve lesions, with radiation therapy playing a particularly crucial role. A comprehensive understanding of leukemic optic neuropathy is essential to prevent delayed diagnosis and subsequent undertreatment due to leukemic involvement of the optic nerve.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 33-year-old female with acute B-lymphoblastic leukemia (BCR/ABL-P190+).
- Chief Complaint: Vision loss and eye pain in both eyes in 2021, progressing to severe proptosis and no light perception in the right eye with reduced visual acuity in the left eye by 2024.
- History of Present Illness: Acute leukemia diagnosed in 2019 with remission after chemotherapy and stem cell transplant. Presented in 2021 with optic neuritis symptoms, intermittent visual loss, progressing to infiltrative optic neuropathy and bilateral optic nerve infiltration by 2024. Unsuccessful remission of vision or progression of leukemia despite intermittent treatments.
- Past Medical History: Acute B-lymphoblastic leukemia, treated with chemotherapy, stem cell transplantation, and immunosuppressive therapy for graft-versus-host disease.
- Personal History: Non-compliance with treatment was noted, including refusal of recommended radiation therapy for optic neuropathy.
- Family History: None reported.
- Physical Examination: 
  - 2021: Visual acuity 20/200 right eye, light perception left eye, optic disc edema bilaterally, blind spot enlargement and scotoma.
  - 2023: Light perception right eye, 20/40 left eye, pale optic disc, optic nerve atrophy (right), optic disc hemorrhage (left), right eye proptosis (22 mm).
- Ancillary Tests: 
  - Repeated MRIs showing demyelination of cerebral white matter, optic nerve thickening (2021), optic nerve infiltration and nodular enhancement of cerebral hemispheres (2024).
  - Visual evoked potential (VEP) abnormalities.
  - Multiple cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analyses remained normal initially but later revealed abnormal primitive cells.
  - Negative tests for infectious/inflammatory causes.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial suspicion of optic neuritis** due to visual loss and optic disc edema in 2021. Infectious or inflammatory etiologies, including neuromyelitis optica and demyelinating diseases, were ruled out due to negative test results and absence of systemic inflammatory findings.
2. **Consideration of isolated ocular relapse of leukemia** based on her history of leukemia and optic nerve thickening on MRI. The initial absence of leukemic cells in CSF made this diagnosis less certain but plausible; this was supported by some symptom resolution following intrathecal dexamethasone. 
3. **Exclusion of chronic graft-versus-host disease (cGVHD)** as no other CNS symptoms, inflammatory CSF findings, or systemic evidence of GVHD were present.
4. **Progression to leukemic optic neuropathy** confirmed by MRI findings in 2023 and finally CSF detection of abnormal primitive cells. The deteriorating vision and optic nerve infiltrative findings on imaging further supported this.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Isolated ocular relapse of leukemia with progression to leukemic infiltrative optic neuropathy.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Isolated ocular relapse of leukemia with progression to leukemic infiltrative optic neuropathy.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. The patient is a 33-year-old female with a history of B-ALL (BCR/ABL-P190+). She was diagnosed in 2019, had chemo and a stem cell transplant, went into remission. But then in 2021, she started having vision loss and eye pain, which progressed to severe proptosis and no light perception in the right eye by 2024. The left eye's visual acuity also dropped. 

First, I need to think about the possible causes of optic neuropathy and vision loss in a leukemia patient. Since she had a stem cell transplant, graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) comes to mind. GVHD can affect the eyes, but typically more the surface, like dry eyes, but maybe not the optic nerve so severely. However, she was on immunosuppressive therapy for GVHD, which might complicate things.

But she also has B-ALL with BCR-ABL, which is a high-risk feature. Relapse is a big concern here. The CSF later showed abnormal primitive cells. That's a red flag. Leukemic infiltration of the optic nerves or central nervous system (CNS) is possible. The MRI findings support this: optic nerve thickening, nodular enhancement. Initially, they thought it might be demyelination (like optic neuritis), but the progression to infiltration suggests something more infiltrative. 

Optic neuritis usually presents with acute vision loss, pain on eye movement, and can be associated with multiple sclerosis or other demyelinating diseases. But in this case, the progression over years, along with leukemia history, makes me think it's more likely leukemic infiltration. Also, the CSF initially normal but later showing primitive cells indicates CNS involvement. 

Another possibility is opportunistic infections due to immunosuppression post-transplant. But the tests for infectious causes were negative. So less likely. 

She also had non-compliance with treatment, refused radiation. Radiation is sometimes used for optic nerve involvement in leukemia. The fact that she didn't get that might have contributed to progression. 

The MRI in 2024 showed nodular enhancement in the cerebral hemispheres, which could be leukemic deposits. The VEP abnormalities point to optic pathway dysfunction. 

Putting this all together: initial presentation in 2021 with optic neuritis-like symptoms, but in the context of leukemia, especially after transplant, and subsequent progression despite treatment. The CSF showing primitive cells later on confirms CNS relapse. The optic nerve infiltration seen on MRI aligns with leukemic cells invading the optic nerves. 

So the final diagnosis here is likely leukemic infiltration of the optic nerves (bilateral) secondary to relapsed B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia with CNS involvement. The proptosis could be due to the leukemic cells causing orbital infiltration or mass effect.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient has a history of BCR/ABL-P190+ acute B-lymphoblastic leukemia (B-ALL), a high-risk subtype. Despite initial remission post-chemotherapy and stem cell transplant, her subsequent ocular symptoms (vision loss, optic disc edema, proptosis) and progressive neurological deterioration suggest systemic or CNS relapse.  

<step 2> Bilateral optic neuropathy in leukemia patients is often caused by leukemic infiltration, chemotherapy toxicity, opportunistic infections, or paraneoplastic syndromes. However, the MRI findings of optic nerve thickening/nodular enhancement, CSF detection of primitive cells, and lack of response to standard treatments (including immunosuppressive therapy) strongly favor leukemic infiltration over demyelinating or inflammatory etiologies.  

<step 3> Proptosis and optic nerve atrophy are atypical for classic optic neuritis but align with direct leukemic cell infiltration into the optic nerves and retro-orbital tissues. The nodular cerebral hemisphere enhancements on MRI further support disseminated CNS relapse.  

<step 4> Persistent CSF involvement (primitive cells) confirms CNS relapse of B-ALL. Non-compliance with radiation therapy likely accelerated progression, leading to irreversible optic nerve damage and vision loss.  

<step 5> The timeline (symptom progression despite leukemia-directed and immunosuppressive therapies) and ancillary test results (MRI, CSF) rule out alternative causes like GVHD-related optic neuropathy, infections, or metabolic/toxic etiologies.  

### Answer:
Relapsed BCR/ABL-P190+ acute B-lymphoblastic leukemia with CNS involvement and bilateral leukemic optic nerve infiltration.
